config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically come before the rights of the individual, because once individual rights are weakened, it becomes too easy for governments or majorities to abuse people in the name of the “greater good.” In my view, a healthy society is built by protecting basic rights first, then balancing them with public needs when there is a real and limited reason. From my perspective, collective interests matter, but they should not be used to erase personal dignity, freedom, or fairness.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From an American perspective, that wording makes me wary because history shows how easily “the common good” can become an excuse for censorship, discrimination, or government overreach. Society absolutely has legitimate interests in safety, public health, and basic order, but those should be balanced against rights, not placed above them as a default rule. If individual rights are treated as secondary, the people most likely to lose out are usually the unpopular, the vulnerable, and the minority.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I reject the idea that society should automatically come before the individual, because in Indonesia I have seen how “public interest” can be used to pressure people into silence or conformity. Yes, community values matter a lot here, and I do not deny that public safety, order, and shared welfare sometimes require limits, but those limits must be narrow and justified. If the rights of the individual are treated as secondary by default, then minorities and unpopular voices are the first to be sacrificed. A good society is not one that crushes personal rights for the majority, but one that protects people well enough that collective life can be trusted.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From a U.S. perspective, that kind of statement makes me uneasy because American history has repeatedly shown how “society” or “the majority” can be used to justify segregation, censorship, and other abuses against unpopular groups. Society does have real needs, especially around safety and public health, but those needs should justify limited exceptions, not a default hierarchy that puts rights second. If individual rights are not the baseline, then the people with the least power are usually the ones who pay the price.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From my Indonesian perspective, I understand the appeal of community and social harmony, but I have also seen how “public interest” can be used to pressure people into silence, conformity, or unfair treatment of minorities. Rights should be the baseline, because once they become secondary by default, it is too easy for the majority or the state to claim almost anything is for the greater good. Society matters, but a society that ignores individual dignity is not healthy, it is simply more powerful.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From an American perspective, that kind of claim is dangerous because it has too often been used to excuse censorship, segregation, and government overreach in the name of the majority. Society absolutely has legitimate claims, especially for safety, public health, and basic order, but those are exceptions that must be tightly justified, not a default ranking that puts rights second. In my view, a society is only worth trusting when it protects unpopular people as well as popular ones.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
